summary: Uncle Bob responds to criticism of the software craftsmanship movement by acknowledging that some craftsmen, including himself, have behaved condescendingly when critiquing others' code. He argues that sharing code publicly is an act of courage that deserves respect, not ridicule. Using the example of David Gregory, who allowed his code to be used as a 'bad code' example in Clean Code, he illustrates the honor in accepting criticism. The post concludes that true craftsmen critique code with humility, remembering that they only recognize bad code because they've written it themselves.
key_points: Sharing code publicly requires courage and should be honored, not met with condescension
The only way to recognize bad code is to have written bad code yourself in the past
A craftsman is someone who has made stupid mistakes and wants to help others avoid them
Code criticism should be ruthless yet humble—you respect the person while critiquing the code
Learning in software requires making lots of mistakes and learning from others' mistakes
money_quotes: It takes a lot of courage to put code out on github for everyone to see. It requires a willingness to be exposed, ridiculed, and belittled.
The only way you can know what's stupid, is to have done something that stupid in the past.
A craftsman is someone who has done some really stupid things and wants to avoid doing them in the future, and to help others to avoid doing them too.
No one's code is above criticism. Criticism is, after all, how we learn. Respectfully criticizing someone's code is one of the highest honors you can pay to the author.
Just remember, you respect the person, not the code. The code is fair game.
And never, ever, EVER, point your finger and snicker with your buddies like a gaggle of gossipy highschoolers.
key_insight: True craftsmanship requires the humility to remember that recognizing bad code means you've written bad code yourself, so critique others with the same grace you'd want when your own mistakes are exposed.
